My last post for today. $LASE finished the day up 160%, but is still continuing it's massive rally in after-market trading where it's up another 11% as of typing this. 

June 12 $1.5 calls went up 10.700% in a single trading session from the bottom of a literal penny. 

Let's see what the morning brings for Laser Photonics. The levels to watch are the Fibonacci extensions and longer term Support/Resistance zones based on Volume Profile. 

I hope you are entertained.

text:
Quantum technologies continue to move from the laboratory toward real-world applications, making conversations between researchers, technology developers, and industry more important than ever.

At APS DAMOP 2026, Margaret Duncan, Stephanie O'Leary, and Corban Murphey are representing Hamamatsu and discussing how advanced photonics, detector technologies, and laser solutions are enabling breakthroughs across quantum communication, sensing, computing, precision measurement, and fundamental AMO research.

From single-photon detection and qCMOS imaging to optically pumped magnetometers (OPMs), LCOS-SLM spatial light modulators, and NKT Photonics' Koheras single-frequency fiber lasers, we're proud to support researchers pushing the boundaries of quantum science and AMO physics. At DAMOP, Hamamatsu is showcasing ORCA-Quest cameras, SPADs, OPMs, Koheras lasers, and LCOS-SLMs for beam steering and wavefront engineering—all designed to enable next-generation quantum systems and precision measurement applications.

If you're attending DAMOP this week, stop by booth 412 to connect with our team. Can't make it to the show? Preview the featured technologies and learn more about the solutions we're highlighting here 👉 https://t.co/qHXrKcKUFB

#APSDAMOP #QuantumTechnology #QuantumComputing #QuantumSensing #Lasers #AMOPhysics #Hamamatsu #NKTPhotonics #QuantumResearch

To quote their website:

"Wayne Tupuola has over 15 years of C-level management. Prior to joining  Laser Photonics, Wayne spent over 25 years of hands-on experience in  fiber laser equipment manufacturing, semiconductor, aerospace  industries."

That being said I'm always happy to see people thinking for themselves and making money. It's definitely extremely volatile on very high volume for it's market cap. By now you should be up so much you could always keep part of your position with a stop loss.

text:
PORTFOLIO UPDATE 6/2
$INTC   $NVDA   $APLD   $COHR   $COHR   $TSLA   $TSLA   $TSLA   $MU   $ARM   $ARM   $ARM   $BE   $BE   $BE   $CAT   $NBIS   $NBIS   $NBIS

Hey guys, still an incredible historic market here. Yes the extensions are real — QQQE more than 4 ATRs from the 21EMA and 7 from the 10wk — but the internal rotation underneath is just as real, and that's what's keeping this constructive.

Market picture:
Tape still in an uptrend above a rising 21dma structure. Breadth is flat though — MCO hooking down, some negative divergence starting to show on the McClellan Summation. Worth respecting.

But look at the rotation — photonics/opticals were digesting for weeks, sitting at a make-or-break, and today they pop hard with big inflow while breadth stays flat. That's the tell. Liquid leaders reclaiming leadership on a flat-breadth day = real rotation, not exhaustion.

Internals three-on-three risk-on. No red flags.

So all in all, I respect the extension but I'm not fighting a market that's still rotating into leadership. When the R/R is there I'm taking it — TSLA and BE today both gave clean spots on the right side of the 21dma. Patient with what's working, manage risk where things are stretched.

On my book:
NER at -0.65%, so a small new exposure risk open — I'm fine carrying that overnight here.
Open Heat at -19.71%.

Adds today were about getting positioned in top merchandise at known-risk spots, not chasing extension. TSLA setting up on the big timeframe with a higher low at the rising 21dma — that's the spot, and TSLA can run without the market. BE same idea, adding to the core at structure with risk defined. As long as my names hold above their rising 21dma structure, I let them work. Risk is known, risk is managed.

Good luck guys ✌️

Today's action:
NEW:
ADDED: BE, TSLA
TRIMMED:
OUT:

How do you have no shame when you have to be run up to speed on very basic fundamentals of photonics?

text:
$MRVL  just jumped more than 24% premarket, but the real story is not the Jensen Huang soundbite.

Huang called Marvell the next “trillion-dollar company” at Computex, and the market did what this market does whenever Nvidia anoints a supplier.

It immediately tried to price the endorsement.

But the better read is structural, not promotional.

AI is becoming a data-movement race.

For the last two years, the market has treated AI like a GPU scarcity trade.

That made sense.

Nvidia owned the highest-value layer, hyperscalers were desperate for compute, and every capex guide turned into a referendum on GPU availability.

But the next constraint is different.

Once AI workloads are distributed across enormous clusters, the problem is no longer just how much compute you own.

It is how efficiently thousands of processors can move data, share memory, synchronize work, and keep the cluster from choking on latency.

That is why Marvell matters here.

The cluster is the computer now.

Marvell is not selling a cleaner AI story because it has a better buzzword.

It sits in the layer that connects the machines: high-speed interconnect, Ethernet switching, optical DSP, silicon photonics, custom silicon, and data-center connectivity.

That is exactly where the pain point moves as models get larger and infrastructure gets more distributed.

Marvell’s latest quarter backs up why the stock is getting attention beyond Huang’s comment.

The company reported record fiscal Q1 revenue of $2.418 billion, up 28% year over year, and guided Q2 revenue to $2.7 billion at the midpoint.

Its data-center revenue came in at $1.83 billion, while Marvell said that business is expected to grow about 50% this year.

Nvidia also put real money behind the relationship, investing $2 billion in Marvell as part of a partnership covering NVLink Fusion, custom XPUs, optical interconnect, and silicon photonics.

That matters more than the trillion-dollar line.

The market is not just chasing “the next Nvidia.”

It is trying to figure out which suppliers become unavoidable as AI factories scale.

Bottom line: GPUs started the AI trade, but connectivity may decide the next leg. Marvell is now being priced as one of the companies sitting closest to that bottleneck.

Huang called Marvell the next “trillion-dollar company” at Computex, and the market did what this market does whenever Nvidia anoints a supplier. It immediately tried to price the endorsement. But the better read is structural, not promotional. AI is becoming a data-movement race. For the last two years, the market has treated AI like a GPU scarcity trade. That made sense. Nvidia owned the highest-value layer, hyperscalers were desperate for compute, and every capex guide turned into a referendum on GPU availability. But the next constraint is different. Once AI workloads are distributed across enormous clusters, the problem is no longer just how much compute you own. It is how efficiently thousands of processors can move data, share memory, synchronize work, and keep the cluster from choking on latency. That is why Marvell matters here. The cluster is the computer now. Marvell is not selling a cleaner AI story because it has a better buzzword. It sits in the layer that connects the machines: high-speed interconnect, Ethernet switching, optical DSP, silicon photonics, custom silicon, and data-center connectivity. That is exactly where the pain point moves as models get larger and infrastructure gets more distributed. Marvell’s latest quarter backs up why the stock is getting attention beyond Huang’s comment. The company reported record fiscal Q1 revenue of $2.418 billion, up 28% year over year, and guided Q2 revenue to $2.7 billion at the midpoint. Its data-center revenue came in at $1.83 billion, while Marvell said that business is expected to grow about 50% this year. Nvidia also put real money behind the relationship, investing $2 billion in Marvell as part of a partnership covering NVLink Fusion, custom XPUs, optical interconnect, and silicon photonics. That matters more than the trillion-dollar line. The market is not just chasing “the next Nvidia.” It is trying to figure out which suppliers become unavoidable as AI factories scale. Bottom line: GPUs started the AI trade, but connectivity may decide the next leg. Marvell is now being priced as one of the companies sitting closest to that bottleneck."}}}, "rest_id": "2061900664234442795", "source": "Twitter Web Client", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "171", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

B.E. Meyers & Company has worked with the largest defense contractors, and some of the smallest most nimble Defense Tech Startups alike starting in the late 80s. 

B.E. Meyers & Co. is a 52 year old company, and for more than 3 decades we've specialized in advanced photonics/lasers. Given our specialty in advanced photonics and laser subsystems, we've developed and delivered products for integration into Aerial, Space, and UAS/UAV/UGV systems. 

Non Disclosure Agreements (NDAs) prevent us from saying who are clients are, but the list is long and extensive: whether or not you know it, you've seen our products! If your company has advanced photonics needs, we are a company that specializes in lasers and we have the experience, capability, and capacity to work within your needs.

Work with the best, work with the most experienced, work with B.E. Meyers & Company.
